title: US-China Trade War Tariff Escalation → Soybean / EM Selloff
summary: The Trump administration announced tariffs on $50B of Chinese goods on March 22, 2018, sparking a tit-for-tat escalation. China targeted US agricultural exports (soybeans, pork), causing sharp price dislocations. EM equities and currencies fell on global growth concerns.
mechanism: China's retaliatory tariffs targeted US agricultural exports — soybeans in particular — as a politically sensitive response designed to hurt Trump-voting farm states. US soybean prices collapsed while Brazilian soy (alternative supplier) benefited. Broader market impact was risk-off as investors priced slower global trade and growth.
causal_chain: Trump announces tariffs on $50B of Chinese goods March 22, 2018
China retaliates with 25% tariffs on US soybeans, pork, and other agricultural goods
US soybean futures fall sharply; Brazilian soybean exports surge
CNY weakens as PBOC allows gradual depreciation to offset tariff impact
EM equities and currencies sell off on global growth concerns
Escalation continues through 2018; full trade war with 25% tariffs on $250B+ of goods

forecast.note: Bearish US soybeans and trade-exposed US ag; bearish EM growth equities; bearish CNY; bullish BRL (ag supply shift); bearish global cyclicals

outcome.realized_note: US soybean prices fell from ~$10.50 to ~$8.40/bushel through mid-2018. MSCI EM fell ~20% in 2018. CNY depreciated from 6.3 to 6.9 vs USD. Brazilian soybean exports hit record levels.
outcome.chart_note: Provisional — soybean futures charts and MSCI EM charts confirm the selloff
outcome.priced_in_note: Some trade war risk priced after initial tariff announcements; escalation pace surprised
outcome.reversal_note: Phase 1 deal reached January 2020 caused partial rebound; full resolution never achieved

title: US-China Phase 1 Trade Deal → Risk-On / EM Rally
summary: The US and China signed the Phase 1 trade deal on January 15, 2020, with China committing to purchase $200B more in US goods over two years and the US rolling back some tariffs. The deal triggered a risk-on rally in EM equities and commodity-linked currencies.
mechanism: Phase 1 removed the most recent escalation threat (tariffs on consumer goods that were delayed or rolled back) and established a purchase commitment framework. While incomplete (most tariffs remained), it removed the tail risk of continued escalation, repricing EM and trade-sensitive assets higher.
causal_chain: US and China agree Phase 1 framework in December 2019
Phase 1 deal signed January 15, 2020
US rolls back 15% tariffs on some consumer goods; China commits to $200B in purchases
EM equities rally; CNY strengthens; AUD, BRL strengthen on commodity/China demand
US soybean futures recover partially on Chinese purchase commitments

forecast.note: Bullish EM equities and EM currencies; bullish CNY; bullish US agricultural exports; cautiously bullish global growth-sensitive assets

outcome.realized_note: MSCI EM rallied ~4% in January 2020 before COVID intervened. CNY strengthened from 7.0 to 6.9 vs USD. US soybean prices recovered partially. COVID derailed the implementation.
outcome.chart_note: Provisional — EM equity and CNY charts confirm the initial relief rally before COVID disruption
outcome.priced_in_note: Deal largely priced in advance of signing; announcement was confirmation rather than surprise
outcome.reversal_note: COVID disrupted Phase 1 purchase commitments; China only met ~60% of purchase targets

title: Russia SWIFT Exclusion → Commodity Surge / Rouble Collapse
summary: The G7 announced exclusion of key Russian banks from SWIFT on February 26, 2022, following the invasion of Ukraine. Combined with asset freezes on Russian central bank reserves (~$300B frozen), the sanctions caused the rouble to collapse and triggered a surge in commodity prices as markets priced Russian supply disruption.
mechanism: Russia is a major global supplier of oil (10% of world supply), gas (30%+ of European gas), wheat, palladium, nickel, and fertilizer precursors. Sanctions and the central bank reserve freeze removed Russia's FX defense capacity and priced in significant supply disruption risk across multiple commodity markets simultaneously.
causal_chain: Russia invades Ukraine February 24, 2022
G7 announces SWIFT exclusion for key Russian banks February 26, 2022
~$300B of Russian central bank reserves frozen by G7 countries
RUB collapses from ~82/USD to ~140/USD by early March 2022
Brent crude surges toward $130/barrel; European gas prices spike to records
Wheat, nickel, palladium, fertilizers all spike on supply disruption fears

forecast.note: Strongly bullish energy (Brent, natgas), wheat, nickel, palladium; bearish RUB; bearish European equities and growth; bearish USD/RUB (RUB collapses)

outcome.realized_note: Brent crude hit $130/barrel in March 2022. European TTF gas prices reached record highs (~€300/MWh). RUB fell to 140/USD before recovering on capital controls. Wheat hit $13+ per bushel. Nickel briefly hit $100K/tonne on LME.
outcome.chart_note: Validated — energy, wheat, nickel, and RUB charts all confirm extreme moves; extensively documented
outcome.priced_in_note: Some premium priced on February 24 invasion news; SWIFT exclusion exceeded many scenarios
outcome.reversal_note: Commodity prices moderated through H2 2022 as Russian supply was partially redirected to Asia; RUB recovered on capital controls and energy revenues

title: US Section 232 Steel and Aluminum Tariffs → Metal Repricing
summary: President Trump announced 25% tariffs on steel and 10% tariffs on aluminum imports on March 1, 2018 under Section 232 (national security). US domestic steel and aluminum prices surged while downstream manufacturers faced higher input costs. Canada, EU, and China retaliated.
mechanism: By restricting imports, Section 232 tariffs created a price wedge between US domestic steel/aluminum prices and global benchmarks. US downstream industries (auto, construction, packaging) faced higher input costs. Countries subject to tariffs (initially all, then with carveouts for Canada/Mexico/EU) retaliated against US agricultural and other exports.
causal_chain: Trump announces Section 232 tariffs March 1, 2018
US hot-rolled coil steel prices surge ~40% through mid-2018
US primary aluminum prices also rise; domestic producers benefit
Downstream manufacturers (auto, appliance) face margin pressure
Canada, EU, China retaliate with tariffs on US agricultural and consumer goods

forecast.note: Bullish US domestic steel and aluminum producers; bearish US downstream steel consumers (auto, construction); mixed for global benchmarks; bearish global trade sentiment

outcome.realized_note: US HRC steel prices surged from ~$650/tonne to ~$900/tonne by mid-2018. US Steel (X) stock rose ~50% in the months following. US auto OEMs cited tariff headwinds in earnings.
outcome.chart_note: Provisional — US HRC futures and US Steel equity charts confirm the move
outcome.priced_in_note: Some metals strength anticipated; tariff rate and breadth exceeded initial expectations
outcome.reversal_note: US steel prices moderated into 2019 as temporary exemptions were made permanent and demand moderated

sources.title: Presidential Proclamation on Steel Section 232 tariffs
